title: Paragliders get the finesse needed to ride the wind
summary: WHEN PETE GAUTREAU SAID THE FORCE OF THE WIND FILLING our tandem paraglider could make it difficult to launch off the slope of Mount Alyeska, he wasn't kidding. We waited for a gust to cycle up the mountain, and when it arrived and he told us to get moving, nothing happened. It was like a dog lunging at the end of its chain -- all momentum and then a jerking halt -- as we tried to move downhill against the force of the wind filling the nylon paraglider like a balloon. Then, as gracefully and quietly as could be, we were airborne, soaring along at 15 to 20 mph. All that awkward maneuvering was rewarded with an eagle's view of Turnagain Arm, some 2,300 feet below. Relishing the comfort of a harness that feels more like a chaise longue than a safety device, it was suddenly clear why so many p…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: In bear country, you just can't be too careful By CRAIG MEDRED The Anchorage Daily News Published: July 17th, 2005 Last Modified: July 18th, 2005 at 03:08 AM HULA HULA RIVER -- No one will ever know how Richard and Kathy Huffman came to be killed by a grizzly bear along this river in late June. It is clear the attack by a healthy, 9-year-old male bear was not the fault of the Anchorage couple. They kept a clean camp. They pitched their tent where they could see bears coming and where the bears could see them to avoid confrontation. They stored their food in bear-proof containers. And they carried a gun just in case all of these bear avoidance efforts failed and they had to defend themselves. They were killed by a bear anyway, and authorities said the dead rafters had done everything right.…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fish didn't get away, but prizes slipped through anglers' hands DERBY: Failure to purchase ticket will weigh heavy on a few fishermen. By S.J. KOMARNITSKY Anchorage Daily News Published: July 20th, 2005 Last Modified: July 21st, 2005 at 04:32 AM WASILLA -- Stephen Phillips has bought a Mat-Su King Salmon Derby ticket every year since the contest began in 2000. But the Mat-Su transit bus driver balked at the $30 price this year. He shouldn't have. Late last month, while fishing Willow Creek, he reeled in a 64-pound king. It was the largest fish he's ever caught. It also would have won the $10,000 first prize in the derby, which ended Friday. Phillips' only solace: He wasn't alone. Every year, one lucky angler hauls in the derby's top prize. This year, 60-year-old Robert MacNeven, a retired …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Tickets $10 for shot at drive on Denali road LOTTERY: Far fewer people applied last year because fee was imposed. By MARY PEMBERTON The Associated Press Published: June 25th, 2005 Last Modified: June 25th, 2005 at 10:13 PM Denali National Park will again be collecting fees from people wanting to take a fall drive in the park after the tourists have mostly gone home. The National Park Service has been holding the lottery to let visitors take their cars, trucks and sport utility vehicles on the park road since 1990. The agency began collecting fees last year to cover the costs of keeping the park open the additional four days in September. The result was that last year fewer people applied for a chance to see the grizzly bears, moose, caribou, wolves, Dall sheep and other animals in the more…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Assembly helps out ski area HILLTOP: In exchange for more control, city will offer funding, loan. By ANNE AURAND Anchorage Daily News Published: June 29th, 2005 Last Modified: June 29th, 2005 at 03:30 AM In an effort to save the Hilltop Ski Area from closing under its financial troubles, the Anchorage Assembly agreed Tuesday night to lend the ski area $120,000 to get back on its feet. The city is also planning to change its lease with ski area managers, a move that could give the operation another $100,000 annually. In exchange, the city wants more control over the area and plans to expand nearby public recreational opportunities. Under the city's plan, skiers could have better cross-country or skijoring trails, parks director Jeff Dillon said. The area needs, for example, a large start-fi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Bird bastion: Alaska known for big game, but ptarmigan and grouse also offer thrill of the hunt By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: July 10th, 2005 Last Modified: July 10th, 2005 at 06:06 AM The four of us had paused along a brook in a Kenai Mountains valley when I spotted snow-white wings clapping and gliding against the yellows and reds of September. Willow ptarmigan. We watched as a half-dozen birds settled along the edge of a scrub alder patch on a hillside above. Others were heard cackling in the brush nearby. It was the break we'd been searching for; the birds were up high, feeding on blueberries that grew fat and sweet along the hills. In a moment we were climbing after the ptarmigan, shotguns at port arms, flare- orange shooting vests bright even against the vibrant fall c…

title: (July 14, 2005) Mat-Su setting borough police plan aside for now
summary: VOTING: Assembly leans toward October 2006 public ballot on the proposal

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kenai, Russian rivers are just the beginning for anglers By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21st, 2005 Last Modified: June 28th, 2005 at 12:40 PM You can feel it as you pass the guide shacks and fly shops of Cooper Landing, hear it in the upper Kenai River as it carries the drift-boat fleets past the Russian River ferry. These things, along with the smell of campfire smoke, mountain winds and salmon that arrive in waves all summer long, leave no doubt that this is an anglers' place. Set in the heart of the Kenai Peninsula, among mountain ranges where rivers are born, Cooper Landing, a rural community about 100 miles south of Anchorage on the Sterling Highway, is the gateway to some of Alaska's finest, most accessible fishing. From here the action begins along the banks of t…
